steps.reveal: initio = in the beginning (abl. of time)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'initium' — 'initio' with -o is ablative/dative
hints.dative: While 'initio' could be dative, here it is ablative of time when
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'initium' — 'initio' is ablative here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'initii' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'initii' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'initii' with -i is 2nd declension

hints.accusative: While 'reges' can also be accusative plural, here they are the subject of 'habuere'
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'regis' — 'reges' is nominative/accusative plural
hints.vocative: Sallust is narrating history, not addressing kings

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'regis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'regis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'regis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: While 'nomen' looks the same in nominative, here it is the direct object of 'habuere'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'nomine' — 'nomen' is accusative here
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'nominis' — 'nomen' is accusative, object of 'habuere'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'nominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'nominis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'nominis' ends in -is

steps.reveal: imperi = of command/power (gen. sg., contracted from 'imperii')

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'imperium' — 'imperi' is genitive singular (contracted)
hints.dative: Dative would be 'imperio' — 'imperi' is genitive, contracted from 'imperii'
hints.vocative: Vocative is not used here — 'imperi' is genitive modifying 'nomen'

hints.to live: That would be 'vivere' — 'habuere' comes from 'habere' (to have/hold)
hints.to dwell: That would be 'habitare' — 'habuere' comes from 'habere' (to have/hold)
hints.to consider: While 'habere' can mean 'to consider', its primary meaning is 'to have/hold'

steps.reveal: habuere = they had (= habuerunt, archaic 3rd pl. perfect)

hints.present infinitive: Present infinitive would be 'habere' — 'habuere' with -uere is perfect indicative (archaic -ere for -erunt)
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'habebant' — 'habuere' is perfect indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'habebunt' — 'habuere' is an archaic perfect form

hints.habito, habitare, habitavi, habitatum: These are the parts of 'habitare' (to dwell/inhabit)
hints.haurio, haurire, hausi, haustum: These are the parts of 'haurire' (to draw/drain)
hints.hibernum, hibernare, hibernavi, hibernat: These are the parts of 'hibernare' (to winter)

translation: Therefore in the beginning kings held the title of power
